item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Flaminglog, instead of replying to your own comments, please edit your previous post using the "edit" function under the "options" tab to the lower right of every comment. That prevents spam buildup, and it's in the rules.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (For the record I did not know about SCP-3455 until after I wrote SCP-3653.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, that one is SCP-3455. The core concept of extra days that we forget about is similar to this one, but I think the execution is quite different.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isn't there another SCP that adds days to the calendar or year, and people know about it during that time, leading to reckless behavior since there cannot be any consequences since it will be reverted at the end of the extra time and no one will remember?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The higher-ups are the only ones allowed to retain memory of the entire 527-day year. Everyone else is still present and conscious with their memories and experience during the rest of the year, and behaves as would be expected of them during these extra days, including upholding any responsibilities relating to containment. The SCPs are also subjected to this time loss, so it doesn't really give them any advantage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I marinated on this SCP for a long time, and added a bit of a better description in the second half to explain why launching 1200 D class at near-light-speed is the containment procedure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a neat concept, the execution could have been a tad bit better, but I like the flip of perspective to the cover scp being the thing that protects you. The reference to 05 using the longer calendar is nice and it makes sense. Would have been a +1 if just a little more work went into explaining the main scp. +0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, but this doesn't work for me. When I saw the article, first thing I really noticed was that there is going to be something that is for Level-4 Personnel only - so I expected a twist. Then I start reading the Special Containment Procedures where, without any real reason, it says that the O5s and Level 4 personnel shouldn't be "cured" of the anomaly. I immediately guessed what the twist was going to be. And without that twist, the article isn't really very interesting.
(Also, if the higher-ups are the only ones that are allowed to experience the entire 527-day year, does that mean that there are about 5000 anomalies that are basically uncontained for more than a hundred days every year?)

Again, I'm sorry, but I think I'll downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not a bad take on this particular idea, enjoyed the jump from a safe, weird little calendar, to a Keter existential threat.

However, nothing really reads very fluently. I found myself having to go back several times to re-read sentence after sentence to understand things. Launching D-Class into space was a nice change of pace right off the bat, but doesn't really have an reasoning or clear purpose.

It just didn't feel like this "4th Dimensional" entity was truly hostile as described, as it is never described as having any sort of personality. It reads more of an occurrence.

I also found it odd how this entity is considered of the "4th Dimension" when the majority of studies theorize that the 4th Dimension is time itself. Therefor why would this entity need to "steal" from living creatures, as Time exists outside of our consciousness?

A good idea but I think to implement it fluently and in a clear way will be difficult.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. Once they're launched, their time is frozen, effectively "banking" it. Launch enough people, eventually it will increase the amount of people in existence by stretching out the existence of an ever-increasing set of the population. However, attributing this to time dilation entirely is kinda wonky, I'll agree, so I'm removing the mention of it but keeping the procedure the same.

2. It's a thing that is unfathomably powerful, and as mentioned by the O5, "it may notice our feeble barrier and directly attack it." This is in effect like a disease developing antibiotic resistance. Overuse of antibiotics will utterly screw over the people attempting to stay disease-free. In this case, the disease is the chronovore, and the antibiotic is the calendar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, uh, the "real scp" had some critical parts missing.

1) what does launching a bunch of Ds into space at nearly light speed do to waylay the Thing What Eats You? Tme dilation (per Einstein's theory) doesn't "give you extra time", it just stretches the time you have.

2) how does inoculating people from the Chronovore make it *more* dangerous rather than starving the bastard?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dunno. I feel like the 'cover entry' could stand on its own and be a perfectly fine skip. (And explain an extranormal event I wrote!)

But the explanation for what's going on is kind of neat. I mean, the "this is the real skip" format has been done to death, and this doesn't do a whole lot new with it, but… I was compelled to upvote by the end. This is pretty good.
